title: Introduction to Video Classification
concept: Video classification is the task of predicting a single categorical label for an entire video clip by capturing both static features and temporal motion.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary advantage of FlowNet over classical optical flow?

options.text: It learns motion features from data, bypassing brightness constancy
It computes flow on audio tracks
It runs without requiring a GPU

exercises.explain: Correct! Because FlowNet is trained on datasets, it is much more robust to real-world illumination changes.

cards.title: What's in the movie?
cards.body: If you upload a video clip to YouTube, how does it automatically suggest categories like 'Cooking', 'Basketball', or 'Gaming'? A video classification model has to watch the entire clip, combining static objects (like a basketball hoop) and temporal movements (like shooting a ball) to label it.

cards.title: Video Classification
cards.body: Video classification is the task of assigning one or more category labels to a full video sequence. The model must analyze both spatial features (what objects are present) and temporal features (how those objects move over time).

cards.title: A movie trailer review
cards.body: Imagine writing a one-word genre review of a movie trailer. You can't just look at a single frame of an explosion (could be action, sci-fi, or documentary). You have to watch the flow of the trailer to decide if it's a 'Comedy' or a 'Thriller'.

cards.title: Action recognition datasets
cards.body: Popular video classification datasets like Kinetics contain thousands of short video clips showing human actions: playing piano, baking pizza, or doing karate. Models are trained on these to label the correct action.

cards.title: More than object classification
cards.body: You might think video classification is just image classification run 10 times. But consider a video of someone drinking water vs. pouring water. The objects are identical (cup, water, person). Only the temporal motion (cup moving up vs. water flowing down) determines the class!

cards.title: Image vs. Video Classification
cards.body: Comparing classification tasks in computer vision:

left.text:
Predicts a label for a single frame
e.g. 'Dog'
Uses spatial coordinates only

right.text:
Predicts a label for a clip sequence
e.g. 'Dog catching a frisbee'
Uses spatiotemporal coordinates

exercises.prompt: What is the goal of video classification?

options.text: To predict a single categorical label for a video clip
To locate every pixel of a car in a frame
To convert a video file into an image format
To upscale a video resolution to 4K

exercises.explain: Correct! Video classification assigns a category label to a full video sequence by looking at both space and time.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Video classification can distinguish between 'opening a box' and 'closing a box' even if they use the same frames in opposite orders.
exercises.explain: True. The temporal direction is a critical feature, allowing the model to differentiate opening from closing.

exercises.sentence: A video classifier must combine spatial appearance with temporal ___ to predict the overall action.
exercises.explain: Actions are defined by motion patterns, which are captured across the temporal dimension.

exercises.prompt: Which of these labels is typical for a video classification model?
exercises.explain: 'Running a marathon' is a high-level action class representing a full video clip activity.

exercises.prompt: Why would an image classification model fail to distinguish between a video of a person 'diving into a pool' and 'jumping out of a pool'?

options.text: Because both videos contain the exact same objects (person, water), and the only difference is the order of the frames.
Because diving is a spatial feature that cannot be rendered in 2D.
Because image models only classify plants.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The static frames of a person near water are very similar. The distinction lies in the temporal sequence of direction. Next: let's study the first major architecture built for this task: C3D!
